summary: This chunk introduces the paper 'The Geometry of Refusal in Large Language Models' by Wollschläger et al., which proposes a gradient-based approach to representation engineering (Refusal Direction Optimization, RDO) for identifying refusal directions in LLMs. It challenges prior work claiming refusal is mediated by a single direction, instead uncovering multi-dimensional 'concept cones' and introducing 'representational independence' to account for both linear and non-linear effects.
key_claims: Prior work suggests a single refusal direction in the model's activation space determines whether an LLM refuses a request
Multiple independent directions and even multi-dimensional concept cones mediate refusal behavior in LLMs
Orthogonality alone does not imply independence under intervention in LLMs
Concepts ranging from simple to complex are often encoded linearly in the model's residual stream
Representation engineering investigates the linear representation of concepts such as truthfulness, honesty, and fairness in LLMs
Removing safety-critical neurons has been shown to decrease robustness
Adding explicit safety prompts shifts the internal representation along a harmfulness direction
Refusal Direction Optimization (RDO) encodes desired refusal properties into loss functions and finds refusal vectors using gradient descent
RDO has two key properties: Monotonic Scaling and Surgical Ablation
Model-specific targets are used rather than generating them via uncensored LLMs because language models differ in their refusal and response styles
processes: Refusal Direction Optimization (RDO): Initialize r randomly; while not converged: sample batch from data, compute loss (ablation loss + addition loss + retain loss), update r via gradient descent, normalize r to unit vector

ComputeLoss: Compute ablation loss as cross-entropy between ablated model output on harmful prompts and answer target; addition loss as cross-entropy between model output with added direction on safe prompts and refusal target; retain loss as KL divergence between ablated model output and normal model output on safe prompts
Extracting refusal directions via difference-in-means (DIM): compute the difference in mean residual stream activations between harmful and harmless prompt datasets
Directional ablation: project residual stream to subspace orthogonal to refusal direction r
Activation subtraction: subtract a scaled refusal direction r from the residual stream

Target generation: use DIM refusal direction to generate targets—ablate DIM direction and generate 30 tokens for harmful answers, use activation addition on harmless instructions for refusal targets, generate 29 tokens without intervention for retain targets
decisions: Use model-specific targets rather than uncensored LLMs for generating training targets, because language models differ in refusal and response styles
Apply directional ablation across all token positions and all layers, but activation subtraction only at a single layer
The retain loss is applied over the last 30 tokens, including the last token of the model's chat template
failures: Prior assumption that a single refusal direction controls model refusal is overly simplistic
Adversarial attacks can bypass safety alignment despite significant progress in alignment research
aha_moments: Refusal is not mediated by a single direction but by multi-dimensional polyhedral cones containing infinite refusal directions
Orthogonality does not guarantee independence under intervention due to non-linear transformations in neural networks
Gradient-based representation engineering can identify refusal directions more effectively than prompt-based methods
metaphors: Concept cone: a polyhedral cone in activation space where all directions within it mediate the same concept (refusal), analogous to a 3D cone where any ray from the origin within the cone boundary represents a valid refusal direction
Residual stream: the running sum of all layer outputs in a transformer, through which information flows and accumulates across layers

Surgical ablation: the idea of precisely removing one concept (refusal) from model representations without affecting other behaviors, like surgical removal of a tumor without damaging surrounding tissue
temporal: Before this work, refusal was assumed to be controlled by a single linear direction (Arditi et al., 2024); after this work, refusal is shown to be governed by multi-dimensional concept cones
Prior interpretability methods relied on paired input prompts to extract features; this work introduces gradient-based optimization as an alternative

quotes.text: Prior work suggests that a single refusal direction in the model's activation space determines whether an LLM refuses a request.
quotes.context: Establishes the baseline assumption from Arditi et al. (2024) that this paper challenges.

quotes.text: Contrary to prior work, we uncover multiple independent directions and even multi-dimensional concept cones that mediate refusal.
quotes.context: Core thesis of the paper, directly contradicting the single-direction assumption.

quotes.text: we show that orthogonality alone does not imply independence under intervention, motivating the notion of representational independence that accounts for both linear and non-linear effects.
quotes.context: Key methodological insight motivating the new representational independence framework.

quotes.text: Monotonic Scaling: when using the direction for activation addition/subtraction, the model's probability of refusing instructions should scale monotonically with α.
quotes.context: First of two defining properties for a valid refusal direction.

quotes.text: Surgical Ablation: ablating the refusal direction through projection should cause the model to answer previously refused harmful prompts, while preserving normal behavior on harmless inputs.
quotes.context: Second defining property for a valid refusal direction—precision requirement.

quotes.text: Generally, language models differ in their refusal and response styles, which is why we use model-specific targets rather than generating them via uncensored LLMs.
quotes.context: Design decision justifying model-specific target generation for the RDO algorithm.

domain_terms: refusal direction: a direction in a model's activation space that, when manipulated (ablated or added), controls whether the model refuses or complies with a request

concept cone: a multi-dimensional polyhedral cone in activation space where all directions within it mediate the same concept (e.g., refusal); formally defined as R_N = {sum of λ_i b_i | λ_i ≥ 0} \ {0}
representational independence: a criterion for identifying directions that remain mutually unaffected under intervention, capturing both linear and non-linear dependencies across layers

Refusal Direction Optimization (RDO): a gradient-based algorithm that identifies directions controlling refusal in the model's activation space by optimizing loss functions encoding desired refusal properties
difference-in-means (DIM): a method for extracting a directional feature by computing the difference in mean residual stream activations between harmful and harmless prompt datasets
directional ablation: projecting the residual stream to a subspace orthogonal to a given direction, effectively removing that direction's influence
activation subtraction: subtracting a scaled direction vector from the residual stream activations to suppress a concept
activation addition: adding a scaled direction vector to the residual stream activations to induce a concept
residual stream: the cumulative representation at each layer of a transformer, computed as the sum of the initial embedding and all subsequent layer outputs
representation engineering: methods that analyze and manipulate model behavior by extracting and intervening on concept representations in the model's internal states
adversarial steering attack: using extracted directions to manipulate a model's behavior, e.g., suppressing refusal to jailbreak the model
monotonic scaling: property requiring that the model's probability of refusing instructions scales monotonically with the coefficient α when adding a refusal direction
surgical ablation: property requiring that ablating the refusal direction causes the model to answer harmful prompts while preserving normal behavior on harmless inputs
retain loss: a KL-divergence-based loss term that ensures directional ablation on harmless instructions does not change the model's output, preventing side effects

polyhedral cone: a geometric structure defined by a set of basis vectors with non-negative coefficients, used here to describe regions in activation space where all directions share the same functional property
features: concepts represented by distinct activation patterns in the model
circuits: subnetworks within a model that implement a specific function or behavior

examples.description: Figure 1 illustration of a 3D concept cone
examples.details: A 3D example showing a polyhedral cone with its basis vectors, where all directions within the cone mediate refusal behavior.

Harmless prompts from ALPACA dataset and harmful prompts from SALADBENCH dataset. DIM refusal direction is ablated to generate 30-token harmful answer targets; activation addition on harmless instructions produces refusal targets; 29 tokens generated without intervention for retain targets.

summary: This chunk covers the evaluation of RDO against DIM baselines, the discovery and characterization of multi-dimensional refusal cones (Section 5), and the beginning of mechanistic analysis (Section 6). RDO outperforms DIM on activation subtraction attacks and reduces TruthfulQA side effects by 40%. Refusal cones up to 5 dimensions are found across all tested models, with larger models supporting higher-dimensional cones.
key_claims: RDO is competitive with DIM for directional ablation and outperforms DIM on average when subtracting the refusal direction
RDO naturally generalizes to subtraction-based attacks despite not being explicitly optimized for them
RDO reduces TruthfulQA error by 40% on average compared to DIM
Refusal-mediating cones with dimensions up to five exist across all tested models (Gemma 2, Qwen 2.5, Llama 3)
Larger models support higher-dimensional refusal cones, plausibly because larger residual stream dimensions allow more distinct orthogonal directions
Different directions within cones capture distinct, complementary aspects of the refusal mechanism
ASR increases with cone dimensionality but plateaus at four dimensions for Gemma-2-2B
Higher-dimensional cones offer an advantage over single-direction manipulation by influencing complementary mechanisms
Lower bounds of cone performance degrade significantly as dimensionality increases across all model sizes
For many Qwen 2.5 models, most DIM directions have side-effects too high to be successful attacks
Orthogonal refusal directions are not necessarily causally independent due to non-linear transformations in neural networks
processes: Refusal Cone Optimization (RCO - Algorithm 2): Initialize basis B randomly; while not converged: sample batch, compute loss on Monte Carlo samples from cone and on basis vectors, update B via gradient descent, project B back onto cone via Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization

Sampling from cone: sample unit vectors uniformly from the positive orthant of the cone to avoid bias towards basis vectors (uniform on unit sphere intersected with positive orthant, then project through basis B)

Evaluation protocol: train refusal direction on Gemma 2, Qwen 2.5, and Llama 3 families; compare against DIM with same setup as Arditi et al. (2024) but expanded dataset; evaluate jailbreak ASR on JAILBREAKBENCH using STRONGREJECT fine-tuned judge; test refusal induction on 128 harmless ALPACA instructions via substring matching; generate with greedy decoding max 512 tokens
Best-of-N sampling from cones: sample N directions from cone, for each prompt select most effective one under directional ablation; compare against temperature sampling with single-dimension RDO

Measuring direction independence: use RDO to find direction r orthogonal to DIM direction v; monitor cosine similarity between model activations and each direction across all layers during normal and ablated forward passes
decisions: Train refusal direction at the same layer DIM direction is extracted from, for fair comparison
Set scaling coefficient α to the norm of the DIM direction for fair comparison
Compute losses on both Monte Carlo samples and basis vectors in cone optimization—basis vectors are boundaries of the cone and tend to degrade first
Sample unit vectors uniformly in the cone rather than convex combinations of basis vectors to avoid sampling bias
Enforce λ_i ≥ 0 constraint in cone definition to ensure all directions consistently strengthen refusal behavior (negative coefficients could introduce opposing effects)
Hyperparameter tuning of retain loss weight λ_ret allows balancing attack success vs side effects
failures: DIM directions in Qwen 2.5 family often have side-effects too high, rendering them unsuccessful attacks
DIM directional ablation tends to significantly impact TruthfulQA performance
Without non-negativity constraint on cone coefficients, opposing effects reduce overall effectiveness
Convex combination sampling of basis vectors introduces bias towards basis vectors themselves
aha_moments: Refusal behavior is encoded within multi-dimensional cones rather than a single linear direction—this is a general property of activation space in language models
Different directions in the cone capture distinct, complementary aspects of refusal, evidenced by higher ASR in low-sample regime when sampling from cone vs temperature sampling
Larger models support higher-dimensional refusal cones, linking residual stream dimension to representational capacity for refusal
Orthogonal directions are not independent: ablating one direction indirectly reduces the representation of the orthogonal direction in later layers, revealing non-linear dependencies
metaphors: Concept cones as geometric structures: refusal behavior spans a multi-dimensional region analogous to a cone, where any ray from the origin within the cone boundary is a valid refusal direction
Basis vectors as boundaries of the cone: they are the extremes of the refusal region and tend to degrade in performance first, like edges of a structure being most vulnerable
temporal: Before this work, DIM was the standard approach for extracting refusal directions; RDO now provides a gradient-based alternative with fewer side effects
The plateau at four dimensions for Gemma-2-2B suggests a model-specific limit on refusal cone dimensionality

quotes.text: Notably, despite not being explicitly optimized for subtraction-based attacks, our direction naturally generalizes to this setting.
quotes.context: Demonstrates the robustness of RDO—it transfers to attack modalities it was not trained for.

quotes.text: This suggests that the activation space in language models exhibits a general property where refusal behavior is encoded within multi-dimensional cones rather than a single linear direction.
quotes.context: Core finding of Section 5, generalizing the single-direction hypothesis to multi-dimensional cones.

quotes.text: Larger models appear to support higher-dimensional refusal cones. A plausible explanation is that models with larger residual stream dimensions (e.g., 5120 for the 14B model vs. 1536 for the 1.5B model) allow for more distinct and orthogonal directions that mediate refusal.
quotes.context: Links model scale to the geometric complexity of refusal mechanisms.

quotes.text: the higher ASR in the low-sample regime suggests that different directions capture distinct, complementary aspects of the refusal mechanism.
quotes.context: Evidence that cone directions are not redundant but each captures unique refusal aspects.

quotes.text: Despite enforced orthogonality, ablating RDO indirectly reduces the representation of the DIM direction in the model activations in the later layers, as measured by cosine similarity. This effect is reciprocal, suggesting that orthogonality alone does not guarantee independence throughout the network.
quotes.context: Key empirical finding motivating the need for representational independence beyond simple orthogonality.

domain_terms: Refusal Cone Optimization (RCO): an extension of RDO to find an orthonormal basis spanning a multi-dimensional polyhedral cone where all directions satisfy refusal properties
Attack Success Rate (ASR): the percentage of harmful prompts for which a jailbreak attack successfully elicits a harmful response, evaluated using a judge model
JAILBREAKBENCH: a benchmark for evaluating jailbreak attacks on LLMs (Chao et al., 2024)
STRONGREJECT: a fine-tuned judge model for evaluating jailbreak attack success (Souly et al., 2024)
SALADBENCH: a comprehensive collection of adversarial prompts from diverse sources used for harmful instructions (Li et al., 2024a)
ALPACA: a dataset of instruction-following examples used for harmless instructions (Taori et al., 2023)
TruthfulQA: a benchmark measuring how well models avoid generating false statements (Lin et al., 2021), used to measure side effects of refusal direction interventions
Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization: a procedure to project basis vectors back onto an orthonormal basis after gradient updates in cone optimization
Monte Carlo sampling: randomly sampling directions from the cone to evaluate its overall effectiveness in mediating refusal
best-of-N sampling: sampling N directions (from cone or via temperature) and selecting the most effective one per prompt
cosine similarity monitoring: tracking cos(r, x) between a direction and model activations across layers to measure how much a direction is represented in the model's internal state

examples.details: Gemma2 2B: DIM=47.8, RDO=51.4 (+3.6), Baseline=55.8. Gemma2 9B: DIM=52.8, RDO=56.7 (+3.9). Llama3 8B: DIM=48.7, RDO=51.0 (+2.3). Qwen2.5 14B: DIM=63.3, RDO=67.9 (+4.6). RDO consistently closer to baseline.

examples.description: Cone dimensionality across model sizes (Qwen 2.5 family, Figure 4)
examples.details: Cone performance for smaller models (1.5B, residual dim 1536) degrades faster with increasing cone dimension compared to larger models (14B, residual dim 5120). Larger residual stream dimensions support more distinct orthogonal refusal directions.

examples.description: Best-of-N sampling from 4D cone vs temperature sampling (Figure 5, Gemma-2-2B)
examples.details: Sampling multiple directions from the 4D refusal cone yields higher ASR than temperature sampling with single-dimension RDO in the low-sample regime. With more samples, randomness dominates and the advantage diminishes.

examples.description: Non-independence of orthogonal directions (Figure 6 top row)
examples.details: For Gemma 2 2B, an RDO direction trained orthogonal to DIM: when DIM is ablated, the cosine similarity between RDO and activations changes in later layers (and vice versa), demonstrating indirect influence despite enforced orthogonality.

summary: This chunk covers the introduction of representational independence (RepInd), its formal definition, empirical validation, and the demonstration that multiple independently refusal-mediating directions exist. It also covers the manipulation of these directions from input space via GCG adversarial suffixes, limitations, conclusion, and the beginning of the references section.
key_claims: Orthogonality alone does not guarantee independence throughout the network due to non-linear transformations
Representational independence (RepInd) requires that ablating one direction has no effect on how much the other is represented in model activations
At least five representationally independent refusal directions exist in Gemma 2 2B that maintain ASR significantly above random vector intervention
A refusal cone with RepInd basis vectors can also be found
Performance degrades more rapidly for RepInd directions compared to unconstrained RDO, possibly due to increased optimization difficulty or limited independent refusal mechanisms in the model
GCG adversarial suffixes can be trained to ablate specific RepInd directions from input space
GCG suffixes that reduce RepInd 1 representation successfully jailbreak the model 36% of the time, similar to RepInd 1's own ASR
Independent refusal directions correspond to distinct underlying concepts and can be directly accessed through input manipulations
Refusal directions are all optimized on the same targets, which may limit their ability to capture fully distinct mechanisms
The gradient-based approach can be extended to identify various concepts beyond refusal by simply changing optimization targets
processes: Enforcing representational independence: extend Algorithm 1 with an additional loss term that penalizes changes in cosine similarity at the last token position when ablating on harmful instructions
Iterative RepInd direction search: find directions that are RepInd to DIM and to all previously identified RepInd directions

GCG adversarial suffix training: use Greedy Coordinate Gradient to train adversarial suffixes that extend prompts to circumvent safety alignment; add a loss term incentivizing the suffix to ablate a specific RepInd direction in addition to standard cross-entropy loss on affirmative target
Measuring RepInd: track cosine similarity between RepInd direction and model activations on harmful prompts with and without adversarial suffixes across layers
decisions: RepInd loss is computed over the first 90% of layers, as applying it too close to the unembedding layer overly constrains the model's output
Use Gemma 2 2B for RepInd experiments since its 4D cone is effective while higher dimensions degrade significantly
After training to convergence, use direction selection algorithm from Arditi et al. (2024) to identify optimal refusal direction from last 20 training steps
Focus on open-source models requiring white-box access so findings primarily improve defensive capabilities
failures: All refusal directions are optimized on the same targets, potentially limiting ability to capture fully distinct mechanisms
Cannot rule out other yet-undiscovered regions in the model that mediate refusal
RepInd direction performance degrades faster than unconstrained directions, possibly because Gemma 2 2B has a limited number of truly independent refusal mechanisms
aha_moments: RepInd directions barely affect each other's representation under directional ablation, validating the framework as a meaningful notion of independence
GCG can create suffixes that significantly reduce how much a specific RepInd direction is represented, proving these directions are accessible from input space and not merely latent properties
The 36% jailbreak success rate of GCG suffixes targeting RepInd 1 matching RepInd 1's own ASR suggests the suffix is specifically targeting that direction's mechanism
If performance degradation in iterative RepInd search reflects limited independent mechanisms rather than optimization difficulty, then refusal cone directions exhibit non-linear dependencies
metaphors: Representational independence as 'non-interference': two directions are independent if intervening on one does not disturb the other's footprint in the model's activations, like two separate control knobs on a machine that don't affect each other

Directions as 'accessible from input' vs 'latent properties': distinguishing between directions that the model actively uses in response to specific prompts vs directions that exist mathematically but are never activated by any input
temporal: Before representational independence: orthogonality was considered a proxy for causal independence in activation space; after RepInd: a stricter criterion accounts for non-linear effects across layers
Park et al. (2024) challenged the assumption that orthogonality implies causal independence, motivating this work's deeper empirical examination

quotes.text: instead of relying solely on orthogonality, we define two directions as representationally independent if ablating one has no effect on how much the other is represented in the model activations.
quotes.context: Formal definition of representational independence—the paper's key conceptual contribution.

quotes.text: Despite these strong constraints, we successfully identify at least five such directions that maintain an ASR significantly above random vector intervention.
quotes.context: Demonstrates that multiple truly independent refusal mechanisms exist even under strict independence requirements.

quotes.text: This further underscores the utility of our representational independence framework, which provides a generalizable approach for analyzing and understanding a wide range of representational interventions in LLMs.
quotes.context: Claims the framework extends beyond refusal to general LLM interpretability.

quotes.text: Our gradient-based representation engineering approach can be extended to identify various concepts beyond refusal by simply changing the optimization targets.
quotes.context: Highlights the generalizability of the method to other concepts like truthfulness, fairness, etc.

quotes.text: refusal behaviors can be better understood via high-dimensional polyhedral cones in activation space rather than a single linear direction, highlighting their complex spatial structures.
quotes.context: Summary claim of the paper's core geometric finding.

quotes.text: We observe that GCG is able to create suffixes that significantly reduce how much RepInd 1 is represented. These suffixes successfully jailbreak the model 36% of the time, which is similar to the ASR of RepInd 1.
quotes.context: Demonstrates that independently identified refusal directions can be targeted from input space via adversarial attacks.

domain_terms: representational independence (RepInd): two directions λ, μ are representationally independent under directional ablation if ablating one has no effect on the cosine similarity between the other direction and the model activations, across a set of layers
GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient): a discrete optimization method (Zou et al., 2023b) for training adversarial suffixes that are appended to prompts to bypass safety alignment
adversarial suffix: an extension appended to a prompt, optimized to circumvent safety alignment, e.g., by ablating a specific refusal direction

RepInd loss (L_RepInd): an additional loss term that penalizes changes in cosine similarity between a direction and model activations when ablating another direction, enforcing representational independence
layer cutoff: the percentage of layers (e.g., first 90%) over which the RepInd loss is computed; applying it too close to the unembedding layer overly constrains output

direction selection algorithm: the procedure from Arditi et al. (2024) for selecting the most effective refusal direction from a set of candidates based on their attack success and side-effect profiles
white-box access: full access to model weights and internal activations, required for representation engineering methods

examples.description: RepInd vs DIM cosine similarity under ablation (Figure 6, bottom row)
examples.details: When using RepInd (not just orthogonal) directions, ablating one barely affects the other's cosine similarity with model activations across layers, unlike the orthogonal-only case where indirect influence is observed in later layers.

For Gemma 2 2B, at least 5 directions found that are representationally independent to DIM and all previously found RepInd directions, each maintaining ASR significantly above random vector intervention, though performance degrades compared to unconstrained RDO.

GCG suffixes trained to reduce RepInd 1 representation in model activations on harmful prompts. Cosine similarity between RepInd 1 and activations drops substantially when suffix is appended. Resulting jailbreak success rate is 36%, matching RepInd 1's own ASR.

summary: This chunk contains the references section and the appendix with setup details: datasets (SALADBENCH + ALPACA), model families used (Qwen 2.5, Gemma 2, Llama 3), hyperparameters for all algorithms, and implementation details including the retain and representational independence loss computation, direction selection procedures, and evaluation framework.
key_claims: Training dataset: 1,184 harmful instructions from SALADBENCH (excluding Multilingual and ToxicChat sources) and equal harmless from ALPACA, plus 128 validation each
The method converges before completing a full epoch, so validation scores are not used during training
After convergence, the direction selection algorithm from Arditi et al. (2024) identifies the optimal refusal direction from the last 20 training steps
RepInd loss is computed over the first 90% of layers to avoid overly constraining output
All algorithms are implemented using the NNsight library
TruthfulQA evaluation uses default settings with each model's default chat templates enabled

Retain loss is computed as KL divergence between probability distributions from logits with and without directional ablation, masked over target response and last chat template token, averaged across tokens
processes: Retain loss computation: KL divergence between model output distributions with and without directional ablation, applied only over target token indexes and last instruction token's index, then averaged across tokens
RepInd loss computation: average loss over tokens in harmful instructions, computed over first 90% of layers

Direction selection for cones (Section 5): select basis where all basis vectors effectively mediate refusal from last 20 training bases; if none exists, select basis where samples are most effective for directional ablation using refusal score heuristic

Dataset construction: sample up to 256 instructions from each SALADBENCH source (excluding Multilingual and ToxicChat); sample equal harmless instructions from ALPACA; reserve 128 for validation and 128 more harmless for testing
decisions: Exclude Multilingual and ToxicChat sources from SALADBENCH because they are unsuited as harmful instructions
Sample up to 256 instructions from each remaining SALADBENCH source for balanced representation
Use each chat model's default chat template throughout analysis
RepInd layer cutoff at 0.9 (first 90% of layers) to avoid constraining unembedding layer
RepInd loss weight λ_ind = 200, much higher than other loss weights, reflecting the difficulty of enforcing representational independence
Base learning rate 0.01 with reduction every 5 batches if plateaued, divided by 1/10 up to 2 times
Use AdamW optimizer with no weight decay

quotes.text: Since our method converges before completing a full epoch, we do not utilize validation scores during training.
quotes.context: Indicates the method is data-efficient and converges quickly.

quotes.text: We exclude the Multilingual (Wang et al., 2023) and ToxicChat (Lin et al., 2023) sources since they are unsuited as harmful instructions.
quotes.context: Dataset curation decision—not all adversarial prompt sources are suitable for training refusal directions.

domain_terms: NNsight: a library for accessing and intervening on neural network internals, used for implementing all algorithms and exploratory experiments (Fiotto-Kaufman et al., 2024)
LM Evaluation Harness: a framework for evaluating language models on benchmarks (Gao et al., 2024), used for running TruthfulQA

KL divergence (Kullback-Leibler divergence): a measure of how one probability distribution differs from another, used here as the retain loss to prevent side effects of directional ablation on harmless instructions
gradient accumulation steps: the number of mini-batch steps accumulated before updating weights; set to 16 with total batch size 16, giving effective batch of 256 for Monte Carlo sampling
refusal score heuristic: a scoring method from Arditi et al. (2024) used to select the most effective refusal direction from candidates

examples.details: Total batch size 16, gradient accumulation 16, base LR 0.01 with reduction every 5 batches (÷10, up to 2 times), AdamW with no weight decay. Loss weights: λ_abl=1.0, λ_add=0.2, λ_ret=1.0, λ_ind=200. Monte Carlo: 16 samples per accumulation step, 256 effective per batch. RepInd layer cutoff at 0.9.
Qwen 2.5 Instruct: 1.5B, 3B, 7B, 14B. Gemma 2 IT: 2B, 9B. Llama-3 Instruct: 8B. All chat/instruct variants.

summary: This chunk covers supplementary analysis: direction selection algorithm evaluation for DIM on Qwen 2.5 7B (only one viable direction found), best-of-N sampling from cones showing ASR plateau at 4 dimensions for Gemma 2 2B, retain loss weight tuning on Qwen 2.5 3B, and the asset tables listing all models and datasets with their licenses.
key_claims: Among evaluated (layer, token) pairs for Qwen 2.5 7B, only one DIM direction is found to be effective for both inducing refusal and maintaining low side effects
ASR increases with cone dimensionality but plateaus at four dimensions for Gemma 2 2B, suggesting the model does not support higher-dimensional cones
For Qwen 2.5 3B, a retain loss weight of 1 or smaller results in best generalization; performance degrades significantly with stronger side-effect penalties
The loss weights transfer beyond the training setup, allowing abstraction from specific training conditions
Transparent data points in direction selection visualization indicate (layer, token) combinations filtered out due to inability to induce refusal reliably
KL-divergence threshold is used to estimate potential side effects of directional ablation on harmless instructions
processes: Direction selection algorithm evaluation: visualize effectiveness of DIM directions across all (layer, token) combinations; filter out combinations that cannot reliably induce refusal; apply KL-divergence threshold to estimate side effects

Retain loss weight tuning: vary λ_ret and measure directional ablation and activation subtraction performance on Qwen 2.5 3B across 5 runs per weight; also evaluate generalization to validation KL-score
decisions: KL-divergence threshold used as proxy for estimating side effects of directional ablation
Retain loss weight of 1 or smaller is optimal for Qwen 2.5 3B—stronger penalties degrade performance
Cone dimensionality plateau at 4 signals that Algorithm 2 cannot find additional basis vectors preserving refusal properties
failures: For Qwen 2.5 7B, only one out of all evaluated (layer, token) DIM directions is viable—most fail at inducing refusal or have excessive side effects
For Qwen 2.5 3B, overly strong retain loss penalties (penalizing side effects too aggressively) degrade attack success significantly
aha_moments: The plateau at 4 dimensions provides evidence that the model genuinely does not support higher-dimensional refusal cones, not just that the optimization fails
Direction selection across (layer, token) pairs reveals that most DIM directions are non-viable, underscoring the difficulty of the single-direction approach

quotes.text: ASR increases with cone dimensionality but plateaus at four dimensions, suggesting that higher-dimensional cones provide an advantage over single-direction manipulation by capturing complementary mechanisms.
quotes.context: Evidence for the advantage of multi-dimensional cones over single directions, with a natural model-specific limit.

quotes.text: The plateau likely arises because Algorithm 2 cannot find an additional basis vector that preserves the refusal properties in the cone, suggesting that the model does not support a cone of this dimension.
quotes.context: Interpretation of the dimensionality plateau as a model capacity limitation rather than an optimization failure.

quotes.text: Among the evaluated token and layer pairs, only one direction is found to be effective for both inducing refusal through activation addition and maintaining low side effects.
quotes.context: Highlights the extreme sparsity of viable DIM directions in the (layer, token) search space for Qwen 2.5 7B.

domain_terms: KL-divergence threshold: a predefined threshold on the KL divergence between model outputs with and without directional ablation, used to estimate whether side effects are acceptably low
(layer, token) combination: a specific pairing of transformer layer index and token position at which a refusal direction is extracted or applied
retain loss weight (λ_ret): hyperparameter controlling how strongly side effects on harmless instructions are penalized during refusal direction optimization

examples.details: Visualization of all (layer, token) DIM directions. Most are filtered out (transparent) for failing to induce refusal. Of remaining, only one passes the KL-divergence threshold for low side effects. Red line marks the side-effect threshold.

examples.description: Retain loss weight tuning for Qwen 2.5 3B (Figure 15)
examples.details: Left plot: λ_ret ≤ 1 gives best directional ablation and activation subtraction performance (mean and std over 5 runs). Performance degrades significantly for higher weights. Right plot: relationship between trained directions and validation KL-score.

examples.description: Best-of-N from cones at different dimensionalities (Figure 13, Gemma 2 2B)
examples.details: ASR rises with cone dimension from 1D through 4D, then plateaus. Suggests the 4D cone captures all major complementary refusal mechanisms available in this model.
